questions.q: In Hindu tradition, what does the Amrita bestow upon those who drink it?
questions.explanation: Amrita is the divine nectar that confers deathlessness, or immortality.

questions.q: From which great event did the Amrita emerge?
options: The churning of the ocean of milk
questions.explanation: Amrita was the supreme treasure churned from the cosmic ocean during the Samudra Manthana, the churning of the ocean of milk.

questions.q: The Amrita is best classified as which type of sacred item in Hindu lore?
questions.explanation: Amrita is a divine treasure, the nectar of immortality that arose from the churning of the ocean.

questions.q: Which physician-god rose from the churned ocean bearing the pot of Amrita?
questions.explanation: Dhanvantari, the physician of the gods, rose from the waters bearing the pot of Amrita.

questions.q: In the churning that produced the Amrita, the two opposing groups who pulled the churning rope were the devas and which others?
questions.explanation: The devas and asuras together churned the ocean of milk in the Samudra Manthana to obtain the Amrita.

questions.q: The Sanskrit name of the churning of the ocean that produced the Amrita is known as what?
questions.explanation: The episode is called the Samudra Manthana, the churning of the cosmic ocean.

questions.q: The Amrita is described in the tradition as having been churned from which body of water?
questions.explanation: Amrita emerged from the churning of the ocean of milk, the Kshira Sagara.

questions.q: Which enchanting female form did Vishnu take to secure the Amrita for the devas?
questions.explanation: Vishnu took the form of Mohini, an entrancing female avatar, to recover the Amrita for the devas.

questions.q: For whom did Vishnu, as Mohini, ultimately secure the Amrita?
questions.explanation: Vishnu, as the enchantress Mohini, beguiled the demons and served the nectar only to the devas.

questions.q: The texts that recount the emergence of the Amrita from the churning belong chiefly to which body of literature?
questions.explanation: The Puranas, such as the Bhagavata and Vishnu Puranas, recount the churning that yielded the Amrita.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, which mountain served as the churning rod during the Samudra Manthana that yielded the Amrita?
questions.explanation: Mount Mandara was used as the churning rod in the Samudra Manthana.

questions.q: Which serpent was used as the churning rope during the Samudra Manthana that produced the Amrita?
questions.explanation: The serpent Vasuki was wound about Mount Mandara to serve as the churning rope.

questions.q: In the Samudra Manthana, the avatar of Vishnu who supported the sinking Mount Mandara on his back was which form?
questions.explanation: Vishnu took the form of Kurma, the tortoise, to bear Mount Mandara upon his back during the churning.

questions.q: When a demon disguised himself among the devas and drank the Amrita, Vishnu severed his head, creating which eclipse-causing being?
questions.explanation: The demon Rahu drank the nectar in disguise; Vishnu severed his head, and the head and body became the eclipse-causing Rahu and Ketu.

questions.q: The Amrita emerged from the ocean of milk, which in Sanskrit cosmology is named what?
questions.explanation: The ocean of milk that was churned is called the Kshira Sagara in Sanskrit.

questions.q: Besides the Amrita, the same churning of the ocean also produced which goddess of fortune?
questions.explanation: Lakshmi, the goddess of fortune, is among the celebrated treasures that arose from the churning of the ocean alongside the Amrita.

questions.q: Before the Amrita and the other treasures appeared, what dreadful substance first arose from the churning of the ocean?
questions.explanation: The deadly poison Halahala rose first from the churning, before the auspicious treasures such as the Amrita appeared.

questions.q: Which deity drank the Halahala poison that arose during the churning, saving creation before the Amrita could be won?
questions.explanation: Shiva swallowed the Halahala poison to save the worlds, an act that earned him the name Nilakantha.

questions.q: The Amrita is contained in a sacred vessel; in the iconography of Dhanvantari, what is he most often shown holding?
questions.explanation: Dhanvantari is depicted bearing the pot, or kalasha, that holds the Amrita.

questions.q: The Samudra Manthana that yielded the Amrita is narrated most fully in which body of texts and in the epics?
questions.explanation: The churning of the ocean is narrated in the Puranas and in the Mahabharata.

questions.q: The devas sought the Amrita primarily to overcome which condition that afflicted them?
options: Their loss of strength and vulnerability to death
questions.explanation: Weakened and subject to decline, the devas sought the Amrita to regain their strength and gain immortality.

questions.q: The word 'Amrita' is formed from a negation and a root; it literally signifies which idea?
questions.explanation: The Sanskrit 'a-mrita' means 'non-dead' or deathless, hence the nectar of immortality.

questions.q: After Vishnu severed the disguised demon's head, the severed head and trunk became Rahu and which other shadow-planet?
questions.explanation: The demon's head became Rahu and his body became Ketu, the two shadow-grahas associated with eclipses.

questions.q: Which two luminaries are said to have exposed the disguised demon as he drank the Amrita, earning his lasting enmity?
options: Surya and Chandra (the Sun and Moon)
questions.explanation: The Sun (Surya) and Moon (Chandra) revealed the demon drinking the nectar, which is why Rahu is said to pursue and swallow them, causing eclipses.

questions.q: The Amrita is reckoned among the ratnas (treasures) of the churning; which wish-fulfilling cow is likewise counted among those treasures?
questions.explanation: Kamadhenu, the wish-fulfilling cow, is among the treasures that emerged from the churning of the ocean alongside the Amrita.

questions.q: In the Garuda lore connected with the nectar, the eagle Garuda seized a portion of the Amrita for what purpose?
options: To free his enslaved mother from the Nagas
To win the hand of a goddess
questions.explanation: Garuda won the nectar of immortality to free his mother Vinata from servitude to the Nagas, which began his eternal enmity with serpents.

questions.q: In Vedic tradition, the Amrita is closely identified with which sacred draught praised throughout the Rigveda?
questions.explanation: The Amrita is closely identified with Soma, the sacred draught of the gods celebrated throughout the Rigveda.

questions.q: While Mohini distributed the Amrita, the asuras were beguiled because she served it according to what arrangement?
options: She poured it only at midnight
She mixed it with the Halahala poison
She hid it within Mount Mandara
She seated the devas and asuras in separate rows
questions.explanation: Mohini seated the devas and asuras in separate rows and, by her enchantment, served the genuine nectar only to the devas.

questions.q: The contest over drops of Amrita is traditionally linked to the origin of which great pilgrimage gathering?
questions.explanation: Tradition holds that drops of the Amrita fell at certain sacred sites during the contest for the pot, giving rise to the Kumbha Mela held at those places.

questions.q: The Amrita belongs to the cluster of treasures from the churning; which of the following that also arose is a radiant gem worn by Vishnu rather than a drink?
questions.explanation: The Kaustubha is the radiant gem from the churning that Vishnu wears on his chest, distinct from the Amrita nectar.
